data.title: Hamilton Insurance Group: Pricing In Strong Fundamentals (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Shares of the Hamilton Insurance Group (HG) have been a strong performer over the past year, gaining 23%. While much of the insurance sector has faced pressure over the last quarter or so amidst fear of slowing premium growth, HG has been able to buck the trend, trading near a 52-week high. Strong results and a favorable hurricane season have helped to buoy the stock. Indeed, since I last covered Hamilton in June, rating shares a buy, the stock has gained 14%, outpacing the market’s 8% rally. Shares are now at my $23.50-$24 interim price target, making it a good time to revisit HG.

There has been increasing concern in the industry about weakening pricing trends. After years of strong premium inflation enabled insurers to recapture margin, we have seen premium inflation steadily slow, which likely means the insurance industry’s underwriting margin has hit a peak. That said, premium inflation, in aggregate, is still keeping pace with inflation. As such, I am not yet concerned about a material compression in insurance margins or earnings power.

Indeed, results continue to be healthy. In the company’s second quarter, reported on August 6th, Hamilton earned $1.55, blowing away estimates by $0.48. Its combined ratio was an excellent 86.8%, leading to $67 million of underwriting income. This was up from 84.4% last year, primarily due to slightly higher catastrophe losses. Still, the level of catastrophes remains fairly low. As a reminder, Hamilton is a diversified niche insurer that operates out of Bermuda, primarily focused on casualty and specialty lines, though property is also 25% of its business. It is fairly balanced between insurance and reinsurance activity.

That said, we are seeing some pockets of weakness. It is pulling back from cyber where pricing is less attractive. Excess & Surplus (E&S) also faces pressure whereas it sees attractive terms in casualty, which is rhetoric very different from peers like Everest Group (EG), though some of its challenges have been idiosyncratic. Its reinsurance renewal saw “some” rate pressure, which is similar to what other players have been saying, though the absolute level of pricing remains attractive. As such, the company used the midyear renewal to increase its property catastrophe exposure. So far, the US hurricane season has been exceptionally quiet. This should be very supportive of Q3 results, though there can be damaging storms in late September or October, which would impact Q4. Still, it appears the US hurricane season will be better than average.

Hamilton is also enjoying idiosyncratic factors that may be supporting pricing and growth. In Q2, gross premiums were up by 18% to $712 million. Being upgraded by AM Best supported $50 million of premium growth. With improved credit ratings, Hamilton is a more attractive reinsurance partner and eligible for more platforms, supporting incremental growth opportunities and potential pricing power. The company is well on track to reach its $80 million premium growth goal off this upgrade, as outlined on the earnings call.

Looking at segment results, Hamilton’s International unit saw its combined ratio improve by 1.7% to 89.3%, thanks to prior year favorable developments. Net premiums were up 17% to $253 million. Conversely, its Bermuda unit saw the combined ratio increase by 6.9% to 84.3%, given a mix shift towards casualty and one large reinsurance loss related to the Air India crash (a genuine one-off in my view). I still view a sub-85% combined ratio as excellent. Premiums were up over 25% to $258 million, aided by the AM Best upgrade.

Alongside a solid underwriting quarter, Hamilton reported excellent investment results with $149 million of net investment income, on its $5.3 billion investment portfolio. Hamilton has a unique investment portfolio. About 61% is invested in traditional fixed income and cash. This portfolio is highly liquid and very high quality, with an Aa3 average rating and 3.4-year duration. The portfolio has a 4.3% book yield. Given Fed rate cuts but positive fixed reinvestment, I see the portfolio yield being fairly similar to current levels over the next ~12 months.

Where Hamilton stands out is its 39% allocation to seven different investment vehicles managed by Two Sigma. It will always hold the lesser of $1.8 billion or 60% of its net tangible assets with the firm. Right now, it has $2.1 billion, so it can withdraw $300 million at any time, and it can redeem with just five days notice. About 70% of its funds are in equities, and over the long term performance has been strong at 13%. This hedge fund allocation is outsized relative to peers, but it has been a meaningful driver of value. Importantly, while this portion of the portfolio is relatively risky, its fixed income portfolio is very conservative, so HG is unlikely to ever be a forced redeemer during a downturn to pay claims.

This part of Hamilton’s portfolio had an annualized return in Q2 of about 17%, generating $87 million of income. Q2 was extremely volatile, offering outsized return opportunities, and this is well in excess of long-term averages. These results can vary quarter by quarter, and thanks to the outsized gain, HG reported a 30% annualized return on equity. Assuming a ~10% long-term return in Two Sigma, investment income would have been about $30 million lower, or about ~$0.25 towards EPS.

When valuing HG, it is important to use a run-rate investment income view, rather than be swung around by each quarter’s return, in my view. Still, thanks to this strong performance, book value is now $25.55. This enabled $35 million of buybacks in the quarter, followed by $15 million in July. $62 million remains on its authorization. Management continues to expect double-digit premium growth, but at a slowing pace. This should enable ongoing buybacks.

Now, in the long term, I still expect HG’s combined ratio to run in the low-to-mid 90% area, assuming a normal catastrophe load, though I expect Hamilton to outperform that in Q3. As such, I believe shares should trade at least 1x book value to 1.1x book value, though the opacity around its investment portfolio likely limits upside beyond that. At a ~$26.25 fair value, shares have about 9% upside.

Accordingly, I am moving shares to a “hold.” I see sufficient upside to continue performing in line with the market. However, given their rally and outperformance vs. peers, I do not see enough upside to maintain a “buy” rating, as future returns are more likely to be market-like. On a pullback towards $23, I would be a buyer, but for now, there is no rush to add HG.

data.title: Anheuser-Busch InBev's Selloff Overly Done, Albeit Expected - Secular Transition At Play
data.description:
BUD Remains A Compelling Beverage Play, Thanks To Its Robust Profitability & Discounted Valuations

We previously covered Anheuser-Busch InBev SA/NV (BUD) (OTCPK:BUDFF) in June 2025, discussing how the worst of its prior boycott might already be behind us, with FQ1'25 results merely attributed to seasonal factors / industry-wide headwinds and similar YoY declines observed in its peers.

Combined with the bottoming owned beer volumes sold, the robust Beyond Beer/ no-alcohol beer performance, and the renewed growth prospects from its diversified portfolios, we had reiterated our Buy rating upon a moderate correction nearer to its next support levels of between $58s and $66s ranges, attributed to the overbought technical indicators then.

BUD 1Y Stock Price

Since then, BUD has indeed pulled back as discussed in our last article, with the bulls defending the $57s floor by early August 2025 and the stock trading sideways at the $60s ranges since then.

Much of the headwinds are attributed to the market's unmet expectations against the underwhelming FQ2'25 performance, as the brewer misses the top-line estimates and just barely meets the bottom-line estimates, worsened by the notable YTD overall volume decline by -2% YoY, the beer volume decline by -2.3% YoY, and the stagnant non-beer volume performance at 0% YoY.

Even so, we believe that the stock sell-off by -14.1% at its worst has been overly done indeed, since BUD's headwinds are merely attributed to the "soft industries and performance in China and Brazil."

BUD's Volume Growth Profile On A QoQ/ YoY Basis

For example, FQ2'25 has brought forth robust volume growth in the North American region by +12.7% QoQ by absolute numbers/ +0.3% YoY organically, the Middle Americas by +10.6% QoQ/ +1.1% YoY, and the EMEA by +16.4% QoQ/ +0.9% YoY, respectively.

These numbers otherwise imply an overall volume growth by absolute numbers by +12.8% QoQ/ +1.7% YoY (after discounting the South American/ APAC regions), with it underscoring the brewer's relatively healthy performance thus far.

This is on top of the fact that BUD's revenue headwinds in Brazil by -1.9% YoY is partly attributed to "adverse weather conditions," the "weakening of mass-market beer portfolios in Brazil, a trend affecting the entire industry," and the "complex economic environment and brewers shifting more focus to premium brands," as similarly reported by Heineken N.V. (OTCQX:HEINY), with it underscoring an industry wide headwind.

A similarly pessimistic trend has been observed in China as well, "as consumers reprioritise value, casual occasions and relevance over prestige" during a sluggish economic market, with International Wine and Spirits Record [IWSR] already expecting:

While beer volumes between 2024 and 2034 are predicted to be flat, RTDs are expected to grow at a CAGR of +2%, sparkling wine at a CAGR of +5% and still wine flat. Spirits are expected to decline at a CAGR of -1%, but excluding baijiu the 10-year volume CAGR is +2%. (IWSR)

Combined with BUD already reporting a declining revenue in China by -6.2% YoY, we can understand why the market has opted to sell first and ask later, attributed to potential growth headwinds arising from two key countries.

On the other hand, it is apparent that the management's ongoing Beyond Beer/ no-alcohol and premiumization strategy have paid off handsomely, as observed in the robust overall no-alcohol beer revenue growth by +33% YoY in FQ2'25, the richer overall adj EBITDA margins of 35.5% (+1.1 points YoY), and the excellent adj EPS performance at $0.98 (+17.4% YoY on a constant currency basis).

These developments underscore that while BUD may continue to face headwinds in select countries, its global megabrand portfolios are likely to remain highly profitable moving forward.

This is especially since the management has already highlighted numerous initiatives to drive renewed growth in Brazil through premiumization and in China through the intensified penetration in the in-home channel while leveraging on its BEES digital sales platform (where over 70% of its domestic revenues are based on).

These strategies may allow the brewer to increasingly ramp up its sales growth as they also scale up the "key innovations such as Harbin Zero Sugar," allowing them to tap into China's booming non-alcoholic market size growth from $169B in 2024 to $291B in 2033, expanding at a CAGR of +6.2%.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

Otherwise, pending growth materialization, we can understand why the consensus forward estimates appear to be rather mixed, with BUD expected to report a decelerating top/ bottom-line growth profile at a CAGR of +3%/ +12.3% through FY2027.

This is compared to the prior estimates of +5.2%/ +14.6% a year ago, albeit still much improved compared to the 5Y historical growth profile at +2.7%/ -2.9% and the 10Y growth at +2.4%/ -4.2%, respectively.

BUD Valuations

The expanded top/ bottom-line growth profile is also why we believe that BUD appears to be cheaply valued at FWD EV/ EBITDA valuations of 9.42x and the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 16.55x, compared to the 5Y mean of 10.33x/ 20.15x and the 10Y mean of 11.61x/ 20.55x, respectively.

This is especially since BUD's FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.76x is inherently discounted compared to the 5Y mean of 2.78x, the 10Y mean of 4.89x, and some of its brewery/ beverage/ energy drinks peers, including:

  • Constellation Brands (STZ) at 3.36x,
  • The Boston Beer Company (SAM) at 1.95x,
  • Molson Coors Beverage (TAP) at 9.29,
  • PepsiCo (PEP) at 5.04x,
  • Coca-Cola Company (KO) at 4.11x,
  • Keurig Dr Pepper (KDP) at 1.82x,
  • Monster Beverage (MNST) at 2.18x, and
  • Celsius Holding (CELH) at 1.25x.

This development exemplifies why BUD continues to offer a compelling growth investment thesis for those looking for resilient, consumer-related stocks, especially given that its ability to increasingly lean into the non-beer market to drive renewed growth as consumer tastes change.

While anecdotal, we have turned to zero alcohol/ zero carb beers for great taste without the hangover/ calorie intake issues, with it building upon our increased awareness of alcohol health impacts as well.

Given the "changing attitudes about alcohol" and the moderating "number of (alcoholic) drinks consumed over the past seven days is 2.8, the lowest figure Gallup has recorded since 1996" in the US, it is unsurprising that BUD has been able to report "high twenties" volume growth in the no-alcohol beer segment in the US.

This is attributed to the popularity of Michelob Ultra Zero and Busch Light Apple, along with "low-twenties volume growth" in the Beyond Beer segment, attributed to the robust demand for spirits-based Ready To Drink Cutwater and Nütrl.

With the International Wine and Spirits Record [IWSR] expecting the "US nonalcoholic volume to gain 16% a year over the next decade," we believe that BUD appears to be well positioned to weather the industry's secular transition over the next few challenging years.

So, Is BUD Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

BUD 5Y Stock Price

For now, BUD has continued to trade sideways between the established resistance levels of $73s and the support levels of $44s ranges since the worst of the COVID-19 pandemic in early 2020s.

This cadence is naturally disheartening, with the stock trading nearer to our base-case fair-value estimates of $59.20, based on the annualized H1'25 adj EPS of $3.58 and the current FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 16.55x.

Even so, given the accelerated adj EBITDA growth by +7.2% in H1'25, adj EPS growth by +18.7% YoY in H1'25 on a constant currency basis, and the management's reiterated FY2025 adj "EBITDA to grow in line with our medium-term outlook of between 4-8%," we believe that the BUD stock deserves an upward re-rating nearer to the peer group P/E mean of ~22x (not too far from the 10Y P/E mean of 20.55x), with it implying its inherent undervaluation against our bull-case fair value estimates of $78.70.

A similar conclusion may also be derived against our bull-case long-term price target of $109.70, based on the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $4.99 and the peer group P/E mean of ~22x, with it suggesting an excellent near doubling upside potential of +75% ahead.

While minimal, BUD continues to offer a decent forward dividend yield of 1.80% as well, with the sideways trading cadence already triggering an improved return profile against the 4Y mean of 1.27%, thanks to the 3Y dividend per share growth rate at +28.8% albeit yet to match the pre-pandemic payout levels.

Even so, we believe that further dividend hikes are likely, since the brewer is increasingly nearer to its net debt to adj EBITDA target ratio of 3x, based on the 3.27x observed in FQ2'25, down from 3.42x in FQ2'24 and 4x in FY2019, with the management likely to engage in further shareholder returns as observed in the -0.7% shares retired over the LTM.

At the same time, readers must note that the healthier balance sheet has already triggered the moderating annualized FQ2'25 interest expenses of $2.65B (-8% YoY/ -28.9% from FY2019 levels of $3.73B) and subsequently, been adj EPS performance accretive (aided by the moderating share count).

As a result of the well balanced investment thesis across capital appreciation and shareholder returns (across dividend incomes and share buybacks), we are reiterating our Buy rating for the BUD stock here.

data.title: Lear: Trading For A Discount, But Too Many Unknowns
data.description:
Company Description

Lear Corporation (LEA) designs and manufactures automotive seats, electrical distribution systems, and electronic modules for car manufacturers. The company has operations in nearly 40 countries and has two reportable segments: Seating and E-Systems. Lear has a current market cap just shy of $6B and employs close to 175,000 people worldwide. The stock is very cyclical, as you can see from the chart below, where multiple times the stock would've returned more than 100% over the past decade (or less). However, the stock has struggled over the last few years, driving down its total return to 25.6% in the last ten years, and a resulting CAGR of just 2.3%.

Quality Financial Metrics

Let's take a look at some important financial metrics and assess Lear's performance in recent years. As usual, I'll be looking for a growing revenue stream, a stable gross profit margin, and a healthy return on invested capital.

The revenue per share for Lear has grown at a solid 6.6% since 2015, but that growth hasn't been linear. The company saw high-single-digit growth during the first couple years of the chart, before a more than 16% increase in FY17. The RPS was flat in the following two years, before the pandemic struck and sank the RPS by nearly 12%. Following that decline, Lear saw revenue growth of close to or above 10%, until 2024, when it rose just 4%. It's worth noting Lear has a share repurchase program, and on multiple occasions during the last ten years, it has reduced its share count by around 5% in a single year.

The gross profit margin was around 10 or 11 percent during the first half of the chart. In 2019, though, it shrank to just 8.8%, and fell further during the height of the pandemic. This metric hasn't recovered to its pre-pandemic levels, and from 2021 through 2024, it has averaged about 7%.

The return on invested capital metric followed a similar path to the GPM, where, prior to COVID, this metric was strong, and routinely close to or above 20%. Again, when the pandemic struck, the ROIC tanked and came in at just 4.3%, and it hasn't rebounded since that decline. I usually look for companies with an ROIC near 20%, so although Lear is capable of eclipsing that mark, it hasn't done so for about half a decade.

Dividend Data

Lear Corporation currently pays a quarterly dividend of $0.77 per share, or $3.08 annually, giving it a forward dividend yield of 2.8%. Unfortunately, the pandemic threw a wrench in LEA's operations, and the dividend suffered. The company has not raised its dividend since 2021...and actually that year it hiked it from $0.25 to $0.50 in September, and then again to $0.77 in December, where it is now. Management has stated they are committed to returning cash to shareholders, but also want to remain focused on investing in the business to sustain more profitable growth. For the time being, it doesn't sound like investors will be rewarded with a dividend increase, so it's likely any excess cash will be spent on share purchases.

Earnings Update

In late July, the company announced their Q2 earnings with a double-beat, Non-GAAP earnings per share came in $0.17 above estimates at $3.47, on revenue of $6.03B, which also exceeded the consensus by more than $100M. Additionally, GAAP earnings per share was $3.06, surpassing analyst expectations by $0.23.

The Seating segment saw a small year-over-year increase in revenue, from $4.45B to $4.47B. This segment benefited from foreign exchange rates, which positively impacted sales by $53M, along with new customers, which contributed approximately $80M. Partially offsetting those numbers was a reduction in the volume of Lear platforms.

The E-Systems segment incurred a non-material decline in sales of less than $10M. An overall decline in production volume impacted these sales the most, but was partially offset by new customers and favorable foreign exchange rates.

The market had a very negative reaction to the earnings call, with shares tanking more than 8% following the release. However, since the call, shares of Lear have recovered, and are up about 1.5%. The full earnings call can be read/listened to here.

The Q3 earnings for Lear are expected to be released on Friday, October 24th, and analysts are expecting non-GAAP EPS of $2.70 per share, with GAAP EPS of $2.06, on revenue of $5.55B. Interestingly, there are 11 analysts that cover Lear, and over the past 90 days, six analysts have revised their EPS estimates higher, while the remaining five have lowered their estimates.

Valuation

The last consideration is whether or not Lear is trading for an attractive valuation, and to determine this, we'll examine it from a free cash flow perspective. Additionally, to give a little more context on how to view the chart below, the estimated fair value is calculated by dividing the FCF/share for that year by the trailing 5-year average of the FCF yield. The discount line is 10% below the estimated FV, while the premium is 10% above, and finally, the Trim line is 40% above the approximate FV.

The custom valuation tool suggests LEA is trading for a decent-sized discount to its fair value, with a current price of about $110, and a fair price estimated at $152 per share. The free cash flow has fluctuated pretty significantly from $1.41 per share in 2021, to almost $6.50 in 2022, and rising even more in 2023 to more than $10.50 per share. The FCF was essentially flat last fiscal year as it came in at $10.48. As you can see by the black line, the share price is well-below the valuation ranges, indicating the aforementioned discount to fair value.

The long-term expected rate of return of Lear Corporation is a robust 17.43%, with the components of that estimate being a forward dividend yield of 2.80%, a return to fair value factor of 5.06%, and an expected earnings growth rate of 9.57%.

Final Thoughts

Over the long term, this stock has struggled quite a bit, but if an investor correctly timed the market, they could've done well with it, although that's not a method of investing I recommend. The financials for Lear were much stronger prior to the pandemic, and haven't quite rebounded since that economic downturn. Moreover, the dividend has been frozen for a few years now, and although the company has a low enough payout ratio to increase their dividend, that seems unlikely for the foreseeable future. The most recent earnings call was rather mute, as neither segment saw any material changes in their numbers. The best part about this company is the valuation, as it appears to be trading at a significant discount; however, the weak earnings report, coupled with a lack of dividend growth and somewhat declining financials, makes my thoughts mixed. I think a HOLD rating is appropriate, as I'd like to see how the Q3 results come in before potentially giving this stock a rating upgrade (or downgrade).

data.title: Salesforce: Get Ready To Catch The Falling Knife
data.description:
Salesforce: Things Might Get Worse Before They Get Better

I guess investors like me are becoming more familiar with companies that appear to have been "left behind" in the race for AI primacy. It's because that's precisely what I believe a Salesforce, Inc. (NYSE:CRM) investor would have felt yesterday as the company released its Q2 earnings release for FY2026. A lot is already at stake for CRM to justify.

Pertinent questions, including whether the SaaS model is facing imminent disruption or whether Salesforce's seat-based pricing models could face complete overhaul risks, have hobbled the stock's momentum over the past year. And despite seeing software peers (IGV) taking out new highs confidently, CRM has suffered another post-earnings decline, with the stock down almost 6% in the aftermarket session.

Hence, it brings me back to some of the salient points that I raised in my last Salesforce update. I recognized the need for CRM to deliver more forcefully to flip the bearish script on its head. Yet, despite posting what I thought was a reasonable Q2 scorecard, the market directly expressed its disapproval with what seems like an arguably light guidance by Salesforce. While I think we can concur that CRM's battered valuations have afforded some bandwidth for disappointment, the market is likely perturbed with the bifurcation between the confidence promulgated by management and its posted guidance.

In other words, there appears to be a disconnect where Salesforce's outlook doesn't seem to fully capture the optimism underscored in CEO Marc Benioff and his team's commentary. Let's revisit some of the salient points that should have appealed to investors, even if they aren't enough to enrapture the market. Salient points on CRM's growth initiatives:

  • Pipeline for the second half of FY2026 is expected to continue growing into the high teens, with bigger deals nearing the 20% growth cadence. These are imputed to the momentum seen in its AI offerings.
  • Salesforce also continues to add headcount to drive growth, with more than 20% AE helping to accelerate its go-to-market initiatives with its enterprise base. Also, sales support headcount has also been diverted to support its sales base, cutting 40% of the support headcount by augmenting with AI agents. As a result, it has also improved customer support conversations markedly, providing more clarity into the future pipeline to help accelerate its AI opportunities.
  • Notably, the growth momentum in its larger customer footprint has also remained robust. Management indicated that data cloud is now a $7B business, emphasizing the 140% surge in customer count over the past year.
  • Multicloud deals have continued to expand, underscoring the potential for cross-selling prospects to improve further. Existing customer conversion is encouraging, as the company indicated that 40% of Data Cloud and Agentforce bookings were attributed to growth linked to prior deployments.

Salesforce's Guidance Has Yet To Convince Investors

Yet, bearish or skeptical investors could lament and demand that such pomp and pageantry should be aptly reflected in the place that matters: Salesforce's P&L.

Salesforce's revenue guidance for the third quarter (up 8% on a constant currency basis) broadly aligns with its full-year outlook (up 8.5% to 9% on a CC basis). I wouldn't blame you if you think the outlook doesn't seem to convey the enthusiasm indicated in management's sanguine commentary.

However, while the SaaS leader upgraded the lower-end of the guidance range from 8% to 8.5% in growth, I think the market is right to have downgraded the company's near-term growth prospects. Clearly, while CRM's operating margins and free cash flow profitability remain highly robust, questions pertaining to whether its business model could face structural risks (even if not existential yet currently) haven't been fully extinguished.

Because, if we contemplate the incredible revenue growth seen in private AI companies while notching record valuations after another, it might seem somewhat antithetical to the typical SaaS investor thinking about Salesforce's participation. And it's not as if CRM hasn't communicated the initiatives and strategies underlining its strategic pivot and forays into Agentic AI.

For that matter, Salesforce believes that it operates a fundamental SaaS layer that is resistant to the disruption potential by foundational AI LLM companies. Given CRM's years of proprietary data advantage and management capabilities, the company's assertions seem credible that it is well-poised to develop and build highly capable Agentic AI systems predicated on its data layer that may not be available to the LLM rivals.

However, I also believe that the primary impediments hindering CRM's valuation upgrade likely aren't so much about it facing short-term disruption threats. Rather, I believe the market is trying to discern whether the increasingly robust adoption of AI offerings through its LLM rivals, and coupled with their ability to subsume even stronger AI capabilities through notable M&A deals, could wrangle the mantle of software leadership from Salesforce.

Note, while CRM's valuation has fallen to the $240B level, the valuations of companies like OpenAI and Anthropic have risen spectacularly, reminding investors that the Agentic AI race remains wide open and up for grabs. And that Salesforce is facing rivals with significant firepower that could rival its ambitions and wherewithal.

Hence, I believe the valuation disconnect is somewhat a reflection of what investors believe could be the start of something worse to come for Salesforce. Or could it be that these investors have taken their pessimism too far, such that have they disregarded that CRM remains solidly profitable and prepared to take the battle to its still unprofitable rivals? I have the humility to admit that I don't quite have a crystal to look well into the future at this point in time.

Yet, I also have the wits and gumption to inform you that when the market takes pessimism way too far against a fundamentally strong and solidly profitable company like CRM, such sentiments shouldn't be expected to persist as the market realizes just how foolish it was.

Buy Or Sell CRM Stock?

And guess what? Although CRM's forward EBITDA multiple continues to hover close to the 13.2x level (two standard deviations below its 10Y average), buyers haven't completely given up on the stock. Note that the stock's long-term uptrend bias hasn't faltered, and I think such optimism corroborates the fundamentally strong thesis of CRM.

Based on CRM's price action, we can easily glean that the buying support has remained resilient at the $230 level. So, if we do get another selloff through the rest of the week, I believe it should afford investors who missed buying the previous pullbacks another opportunity to load up.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

data.title: Molson Coors: Expect More Weakness Ahead
data.description:
Molson Coors Beverage Company (TAP) still has a weak outlook ahead after the company lowered its FY2025 guidance for a second time. The beer industry continues to face softness as young people drink less, on top of which Molson Coors is losing market share and seeing cost inflation from aluminum tariffs.

I initiated the stock at a Hold rating in my initial May article, titled “Molson Coors Q1: Caught In Worrisome Sales Trends.” The sales performance hasn’t since improved, leading the stock to lose a further -8% of its value. The S&P 500 (SP500) has returned 14% in the same period.

Molson Coors’ Weakness Continues

Molson Coors still has a very weak immediate earnings growth outlook. The Q2 report showed a -2.6% constant currency sales decline to $3.20 billion, seen across Molson Coors’ primary sales markets in the Americas, EMEA, and the APAC region. The performance reflects a sharp -7.0% decline in financial volume, in part offset by good increases in pricing and favorable developments in the sales mix as Molson Coors is going through portfolio premiumization. Earnings have ultimately followed sales, as despite strong pricing gains, adjusted pre-tax income declined by -0.8% in constant currency to $532 million.

The outlook remains similarly weak, and Molson Coors already had to lower its 2025 guidance for a second time. The company now assumes 2025 sales to decline by -3% to -4% in constant currency, and that adjusted pre-tax income will show a much sharper -12% to -15% decline through sales deleverage and cost inflation. As a major cost inflation item, higher tariffs have caused additional pressure on earnings after Trump doubled the aluminum tariff to 50%. The Midwest premium spot price surged in June, and as aluminum is a major cost item for breweries, Molson Coors expects incremental aluminum costs to amount to $40-55 million on an annual basis.

A concerning sales trend has still been seen on an industry-wide basis. Constellation Brands (STZ) very recently also cut its FY2026 guidance to -6% to -4% comparable sales growth during dampened consumer demand for beer. Most other companies in the industry haven’t been unaffected by weak beer consumption either; Molson Coors’ updated guidance assumes the U.S. beer industry to be down -4% to -6% during H2.

Concerningly for the industry, while Molson Coors notes the trend to be cyclical and many other major breweries have also related weak demand to macroeconomic pressure, consumer spending has remained healthy in the U.S., with 1.2% real growth in sales to private domestic purchasers during Q2. Declining beer sales reflect weakening alcohol consumption rather than macroeconomic pressure, as younger people generally drink less. The industry’s pressure seems to be secular rather than transitory, and an improvement may not be seen in 2026 or afterwards if the social trend doesn’t bend. As I noted in a recent article on spirits giant Diageo (DEO), only a quarter of U.S. consumers have cited saving money as a reason for moderating alcohol consumption.

At the same time, Molson Coors’ weak outlook underlines even more concerning trends for the company. Molson Coors has lost market share in the U.S. and notes a heightened competitive landscape in the EMEA & APAC regions as well—Molson Coors’ growth outlook is even weaker than the industry’s if the company doesn’t manage to stabilize its market share.

The concerns leave Molson Coors with a concerning long-term outlook. Past the weak FY2025 guidance, the industry’s secular decline and Molson Coors’ struggles in maintaining market share look to weigh on the long-term earnings performance as well.

TAP Stock’s Valuation Already Doesn’t Assume Much

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Molson Coors. I now assume even slower mid-term growth than previously, ultimately still stabilizing at a weak 0.5% annual revenue growth. The expected pace is slower than general inflation over the long term, factoring in the industry’s secular weakness.

Sales deleverage and tariffs look to pressure Molson Coors’ margins. I now assume the EBIT margin to stabilize at 13.5%, down considerably from 15.6% in FY2024. I still assume Molson Coors to convert earnings well into free cash flow.

The estimates put Molson Coors’ fair value at $55.5, 11% above the stock price at the time of writing; not much is priced in at a 9.3 forward P/E. Even so, the moderate upside doesn’t make Molson Coors an attractive investment. The estimated upside is very moderate, and the DCF model already assumes sales growth to stabilize at a better level over the long term than the current outlook suggests.

The fair value estimate is down from $66.9 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 7.47%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a 3.75% interest rate, annualized from Molson Coors’ Q2 interest expenses and debt. I again assume a 40% long-term debt-to-equity ratio.

For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.22% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.62% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent July estimate for the U.S. I have kept the beta estimate at 0.74. With an ESG add-on of 1.5% and a liquidity premium of 0.2%, the cost of equity stands at 9.34% and the WACC at 7.47%.

Takeaway

Molson Coors’ earnings outlook is incredibly weak, pressured by a number of industry- and company-specific factors. An improvement isn’t in sight. Even though the stock’s valuation doesn’t assume much, I don’t believe Molson Coors to be an attractive investment at the current price. I maintain a Hold rating for TAP stock.

data.title: Nvidia Is Still In The Early Innings Of The AI Revolution
data.description:
NVIDIA Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) continues to realize exceptionally strong growth as AI adoption expands across enterprises and consumers. With a growing capital investment outlay for the next generation of computing power and physical industrial automation and robotics, Nvidia remains the clear leader to participate in this growth. I believe shares should be priced at $278/share at 23.35x eFY27 price/sales; I am reiterating my Strong Buy rating.

You can read my previous coverage of Nvidia here:

Nvidia Stands To Benefit From Positive U.S. And China Trade Talks

Nvidia Operations

Nvidia reported significant top-line growth in Q2’25, with Data Center driving performance. Inclusive of the lost $4b in revenue from the Chinese market, Data Center would have expanded by 72%, well above the reported 56% segment growth rate when compared to the previous year’s quarter. Though compute sales growth fell to 50% in Q2’26, networking revenue, which includes Spectrum-X Ethernet, InfiniBand, and NVLink, grew at a 98% rate when compared to the previous year’s quarter.

As part of the transition to GB300 architecture, Nvidia has ramped up production and is set to produce roughly 1,000 GB300 server racks per week, with additional capacity coming online in Q3’26.

Nvidia realized strong growth across all segments in addition to the Data Center market segment. Growth in Gaming and Professional Visualization accelerated to 49% and 32% year-over-year growth, driven by shipments of GeForce GPUs and RTX workstation GPUs. Automotive growth also remained strong at 69% when compared to the previous year’s quarter, driven by content for self-driving vehicles.

Management indicated a $3-4T addressable market by the end of the decade, driven by data center build-outs and enterprise AI adoption. Though this is a lofty figure considering the current market environment, I believe that there is some weight behind the figures. The Wall Street Journal recently reported that grid operators are receiving requests for nearly 400GW of electricity, much of which is for data center development. Though these figures may not be concrete as logistics are worked out amongst cloud service providers [CSPs] and other data center hosts, this is suggestive of a significant uplift in demand for data center capacity for the coming years.

For example, OpenAI and Oracle Corporation (ORCL) are set to deploy 64,000 Nvidia GB200s at the Abilene, Texas site by the end of 2026 as part of the Stargate project. More recently, the two companies entered into an agreement for an additional 4.5GW of data center capacity in the US, setting the stage for substantial growth to come. Accordingly, the 5GW of data center capacity will run over 2 mm chips. Though the compute capacity is likely going to be a mix of CPU and GPU chips, this can be a major uplift for Nvidia for the coming years, suggesting that capacity demand is expected to remain strong.

Internationally, President Trump came to terms with Nvidia for the sale of the H20 to Chinese customers with a 15% export duty. Accordingly, Nvidia is working with the US Government in reviewing licenses for the sale of the H20 GPUs, beginning the process to revamp shipments. Despite the turn in domestic regulation, the Chinese government announced that Chinese technology companies should stop using H20 chips, suggesting that their use may pose the risk of information leakage. Accordingly, the Chinese government hasn’t imposed an outright ban on the H20 outside of military facilities. Overall, I suspect that this is merely a geopolitical stunt between President Trump and President Xi Jinping of China as trade negotiations intensify. Assuming discussions move forward according to plan, Nvidia is expecting to ship $2-5b in H20 revenue in eQ3’26. Given the relatively passive language of the Chinese government, I suspect Nvidia will have the ability to ship some volumes of the H20 in eQ3’26.

On the domestic front, I believe Nvidia has a strong opportunity in virtual and physical AI, particularly driven by the incoming $1.15T in new investments resulting from the recent trade agreements with the European Union [EU] and Japan. I suspect a proportional amount of investments will go towards industrials and manufacturing, driving the need for accelerated computing for factory automation and industrial robotics for staff augmentation. This could potentially be a leading opportunity for Nvidia in the coming years, driving significant expansion for AI compute capacity as well as Jetson Thor, Nvidia’s AI robotics computing platform.

Nvidia Financial Position

Looking ahead, I’m forecasting Nvidia to generate $58b in net revenue with an adjusted EPS of $1.33/share for eQ3’26. Given the robust growth across segments, I’m anticipating Nvidia to continue to realize durable strength for Data Center, Gaming, and Professional Visualizer as CSPs, consumers, and enterprises adopt accelerated computing infrastructure, particularly with the next-generation Blackwell technology. I’m also forecasting that margins will strengthen in the back half of eFY26 as production volumes ramp up to meet market demand.

Looking to the balance sheet, Nvidia closed Q2’26 with $56.8b in cash & equivalents and $8.5b in debt on the balance sheet for a net cash position of $48b. The increase in cash can be attributed to the organization generating $13.45b in free cash flow in the quarter. Uses of cash in the quarter can be attributed to working capital investments growing days receivable and inventories to 61 days and 111 days, respectively. Nominally, inventories grew by 32% to $15b as a result of inventory build to support the Blackwell and Blackwell Ultra ramp-up.

Risks Related to Nvidia

Bull Case

Nvidia remains the clear leader for AI-accelerated infrastructure, expanding its presence in data center networking technology as AI compute scales across CSPs and enterprise customers. Gaming and Professional Visualization are ramping up as consumer and enterprise edge AI demand grows; a continued ramp-up in edge AI can result in durable growth across these market segments.

Bear Case

NVDA shares’ valuation may appear relatively extended at 39.61x EV/aEBITDA, potentially leading to some investor hesitancy. Nvidia is dependent on Chinese & US trade relations improving in order to service the growing Chinese AI market; if Nvidia remains outside of the Chinese market, the organization may miss out on multiple billions of dollars in revenue.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

In Q2’26, the Board of Directors approved a $60b share repurchase authorization, bringing the total available capital to $74.7b set aside for share repurchases.

NVDA shares currently trade at 25.35x price/sales on a trailing twelve-month basis, well above market peers. Despite the high trading premium, I believe NVDA shares’ valuation is justified given the organization’s market dominance and growth trajectory.

Valuing NVDA shares, I believe shares should be priced at $278/share at 23.35x eFY27 price/sales; I am reiterating my Strong Buy rating.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle, with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market cap and relative multiple.

data.title: Wynn Resorts: Macau Stability And UAE Growth Drive Upside Potential
data.description:
Wynn Resorts, Limited (NASDAQ:WYNN) is a high-end casino resort operator and developer whose stock price has significantly outperformed the broader market, with a 45% year-to-date increase. Wynn's Macau properties were a major headwind for the company, as they had underperformed, but it seems these concerns are in the rearview mirror with stable Macau results.

At a forward P/E of around 27, the market seems to have priced in the recovery, and the positive sentiment has returned. Despite valuations appearing to be slightly elevated, I think the company is well-positioned to unlock further upside potential with the bottom line growth expectations and UAE expansion.

The diluted EPS forward growth rate at 11% indicates that the company expects significant bottom line growth, which should make valuations appear more compelling and accelerate further multiple expansion.

I think Wynn appears to be a compelling long-term opportunity for investors, and I rate it as a Buy with a $145 price target over the next 12 months. UAE expansion supports another top line growth cycle, which may be rewarded sooner rather than later.

Q2 2025: Double Miss, But Tailwinds Ahead

On August 7, the company reported its Q2 2025 results. The markets proved to be forward-looking—despite the double miss, Wynn stock closed almost flat for the day at $107.23 versus the open price of $107.62.

The company reported total revenue of $1.74 billion, which was up 0.3% year-over-year. Las Vegas performance was significant, with a record property-level EBITDAR at $234 million and total revenue of $638 million for the region, reflecting a 2% year-over-year increase.

Encore Boston Harbor reported $215 million in revenue, up 1% YoY. The Macau business remained stable, with Wynn Palace posting $539 million in revenue and Wynn Macau reporting $343 million for the quarter.

Wynn Resorts reported $66 million in net income for the quarter, about a 41% decline year-over-year, which led to EPS of $0.64, nearly a 30% decline in bottom line compared to the same quarter last year.

The company disclosed its strategic update regarding Wynn Al Marjan Island in the UAE. Wynn reported an additional $58 million investment throughout Q2, and the company expects the opening in early 2027. I think this may unlock another growth cycle for the high-end casino operator, which, from a long-term perspective, appears compelling.

The company continues with a shareholder-friendly capital approach with share repurchases. Wynn returned $158 million and declared a $0.25 per-share dividend. The high-end casino developer ended the quarter with around $1.98 billion in cash and total obligations of over $10.5 billion, indicating its medium-leverage stance at nearly 5x EBITDAR leverage.

This was a mixed quarter for the company, but the market appreciated significant UAE progress and overall resilience. Macau and Boston businesses remained stable, while Las Vegas recorded substantial results in terms of property-level EBITDAR. It seems the company's main worries are in the rearview mirror, and the market is focusing on tailwinds ahead with improved sentiment.

Wynn Is Fairly Priced, But Bottom Line Growth Is Overlooked

The company currently trades at a forward P/E of 26.96, which is fairly priced, considering its historical valuation levels. One could argue that Wynn has more room for expansion, as before Macau concerns, the company traded at an above 30x earnings multiple. I think sentiment around UAE expansion and built-up expectations could drive the multiple expansion further to higher-end levels.

At a forward EV/Sales, Wynn is valued at 3.18, nearly 2.6x higher compared to the industry average of 1.32. It may appear overvalued, but this metric doesn't account for the UAE venture, and with expected revenue increase, the company may trade at a more market-typical ratio. At a forward EV/EBITDA of 10.11, a roughly 6% undervaluation compared to the average company in the industry at 10.78 offsets the argument of the company being expensive, as indicated; there is more room for multiple expansion.

The Price/Sales (forward) at 1.84 appears to be trading at a premium versus the sector median of 0.97, but from the Price/Cash Flow (forward) point of view, at 10.62 versus peers' 11.53, it shows potential undervaluation. Valuation brings a balanced view, which suggests it is fairly priced.

The company is a medium-leverage business with total obligations of $12.17 billion. Wynn Resorts operates in a capital-intensive industry; thus, elevated levels are a typical business trait. The company has $1.98 billion in cash, which makes the outlook more compelling.

Nevertheless, it costs around $160 million every quarter for the company to service its debt. With the expectations of the Fed lowering interest rates, Wynn may face another tailwind, as it may be able to refinance obligations on more favorable conditions. In accordance, it would result in an interest expense decline and help the bottom line growth.

The company's revenue has declined by 2% over the past 12 months, but the market overlooked it due to both complete business recovery from a significant COVID top line decline and expectations of a revenue increase due to expansion in the UAE. Wall Street analysts expect around 4% annual revenue growth over the next two years, which I think is a rational and achievable estimate.

The forward EBIT growth rate at 14.89% indicates significant outperformance compared to the industry's average of 4.43%. It signals high-quality operations, high profitability, and operational efficiency. This also implies that Wynn Resorts has competitive advantages and a strong business model.

The diluted EPS growth rate at 11.16%, nearly 82% above average peer outperformance at 6.13%, is significant and I believe is overlooked by the market and not fully priced in yet. This comes in line with analysts' estimates, as they expect double-digit growth of the bottom line through FY2027. At FY2026 EPS estimate of $5.71, the company would be undervalued compared to the average S&P 500 company, which trades at a 23x earnings multiple.

I think that it is likely for the company to achieve this estimate due to operational efficiencies, business stability, and the buyback program. At the FY2026 EPS, applying a 25.5x multiple, it would result in my expected price target of $145 over the next 12 months. I think that if the average company in the S&P 500 grows its bottom line at 9% annually, twice the growth pace should be rewarded with a higher premium; thus, a 10% elevation above market-average P/E appears reasonable.

Profitability supports my bullish stance and a bright outlook for the company. It is a highly profitable business that operates at nearly 77% higher gross profit margins (TTM) of 68.32% compared to the industry's average of 38.41%.

The company operates at a 24.46% EBITDA margin (TTM), which is roughly 2.1x higher than peers' at 11.16%. The net income margin (TTM) of 5.51% suggests nearly 31% outperformance compared to the industry's average of 4.21%, supporting my bullish stance and sustainable bottom line growth.

Wynn earned $1.30 billion in cash from operations (TTM), which is significant compared to the industry's average of only $288 million. It has led to $19.28 in cash per share (TTM) versus peers' $2.62, indicating 6x outperformance.

Wynn Resorts delivered a mixed performance over the past 12 months, but shareholders should be satisfied with complete business recovery and expansion in the UAE. The company remains highly profitable and operates at above market-average gross profit margins. Wynn is expected to significantly increase its bottom line, which should help valuation and may achieve my reasonable price target of $145.

Main Risks And Concerns

Wynn Resorts faces several risks and concerns that could substantially impact its stock price performance in the near term.

Macau revenue indicates stability but also raises questions of stagnation. Over the past 5 quarters, revenue from Macau operations has flatlined in the range of $860 million to $885 million. It may indicate that recovery has peaked and growth may be limited.

Wynn Resorts' profitability is highly correlated with VIP hold. This quarter was a great example, as despite stable revenue, it was a less profitable quarter due to weak hold of VIP—2.86% versus 4.1% the same quarter last year. This brings uncertainty to the business, and volatility tends to erode investor sentiment. Also, huge volatility swings may affect future share repurchases, as if the earnings were to soften, management may cut back on buybacks.

The company faces execution risk tied to UAE expansion. Any uncertainty around construction costs, delays, or potential regulatory challenges may weigh on future outlook and stress the stock price.

Wynn is a discretionary business and has cyclical exposure. If the consumer weakens or we face a significant global slowdown, the company may see a decline in demand, which would drive multiple contractions.

On the other hand, if none of the concerns were to materialize, Wynn Resorts has a bright outlook with UAE expansion and significant bottom line growth. The stock is priced fairly, and I think the market hasn't fully accounted for its projected double-digit EPS growth over the next years. If it succeeds, the company presents a compelling long-term opportunity to consider.

Conclusion: Tailwind Suggests Potential Market Outperformance

I think that Wynn Resorts is a high-quality business with a prestigious brand that offers high-end services to its clients. The company operates at above-market-average gross profit margins, which maintains the positive outlook for significant bottom line growth.

Despite recent-year headwinds, the company's revenue completely recovered, and with the UAE expansion, it is expected to unlock a new growth cycle. I think it presents an attractive opportunity for investors who favor a high-end business model with stable global revenue and continued expansion.

That is why I think Wynn is a Buy with a $145 price target over the next 12 months, indicating nearly 16% upside and broader market outperformance. I believe tailwinds are not fully priced in and may drive continued multiple expansion.

18 Wall Street analysts suggest a $127.47 price target over the next 12 months, implying 1.55% upside potential. I am more optimistic, as I believe great execution could make UAE expansion a success, and the market may start pricing in this sooner rather than later.

data.title: Cal-Maine Foods: Cash Flow Consistency And Dividends
data.description:
Cal-Maine Foods (NASDAQ:CALM) is the largest producer and supplier of eggs in the US. Despite being in a less trendy industry, the stock price performed very well in a year-to-date comparison with the S&P 500, besides counting on a strong dividend yield, low beta, and a Strong Buy Quant Rating. The firm’s financial performance is, on the other hand, very influenced by external factors that may directly regulate the volatile price of the egg. On the bright side, the firm shows consistent financial discipline to benefit from higher prices for the benefit of stockholders, magnifying its return.

Current Financials Support a Buy

In my research, I compare several target firms that are selected based on an appropriate investment thesis. Right now, some of the relevant theses include Artificial Intelligence Hardware and Applications, Infrastructure and Cybersecurity, among others. Before the interest rate rising cycle some years ago, Food Staples were a relevant investment thesis due to the potential benefit of increased demand in inflationary environments, as many of these products are Giffen Goods. In 2025, with stubborn inflation and the risk of inflation reignition driven by tariffs, this thesis was reincorporated into the research.

Cal-Maine performed surprisingly well in the comparison, mostly related to its true total return and its momentum measures, placing well above its peers in the Food Staples thesis and aligned with firms with strong storytelling, like Nvidia (NVDA). Past results, nevertheless, are highly influenced by the revenue volatility experienced by Cal-Maine, as depicted in Figure 1.

Revenue peaks and troughs basically mirror price behavior. Figure 2 illustrates price fluctuations in the US, with clear peaks related to supply constraints, generally tied to Highly Pathogenic Avian Influenza (HPAI) outbreaks. For the reported 2025 year (ended in May/2025), the volume sold was 1,282 million dozen, an 11.6% increase year-over-year. Revenue in full year 2025, on the other hand, increased 83.2%, with the participation of conventional eggs increasing in the product mix. One obvious conclusion is that the price was the main driver of the revenue boost. A second possible conclusion is that customers are migrating to cheaper options in response to general price levels.

Cal-Maine’s growth, moreover, is unique when evaluated with other food staples firms, as shown in Figure 3. It also delineates the stock performance differential of the firm compared to its peers. Another relevant information is that bottom-line performance is fully dependent on sales, meaning that the company keeps its operating leverage profile by not indulging in higher expenses as revenues increase. Figure 4 portrays margin evolution.

Compared to the peer group, Cal-Maine clearly depicts a greater margin volatility, especially when compared to Kraft Heinz (KHC) and General Mills (GIS), with a current situation above peers. In Figure 5, this comparison is depicted, including the goodwill impairment that affected the result of Kraft Heinz, not covered in this article.

In terms of the balance sheet, Cal-Maine presents a strong situation, both in terms of liquidity and solvency. In the first case, the Current Ratio above 6 demonstrates a comfortable liquidity position. The company also has no financial leverage or goodwill, indicating that the full earnings variation arises from the business and not the financial structure, and that growth is not fueled by pricey acquisitions. Please refer to Figure 6 in this analysis.

The resulting operating cash generation is consistent with previous findings, linked mainly to the sales operation and a quasi-fixed efficiency structure. The consequence of free cash generation is that the company is able to fund capex, dividends, and increase cash position. The firm’s cash jumped to US$1,392 million from US$174 million in 2022. Figures 7 and 8 illustrate the operating cash and the cash generation pattern. In Figure 8, the analysis covers the last 4 years, and the values are scaled by the net sales for comparison among companies. In Figure 9, it is possible to notice the interesting position that Cal-Maine (the red dot) occupies in regard to other targets under research.

Moreover, when evaluating Cal-Maine in comparison to the other companies under research, Figure 10 demonstrates its performance on all scaled metrics, from sales to cash flow.

Future Prospects and Demand Forecast

According to the company, the main demand driver is population growth. Other sources may link demand increase to a combination of price and nutritional features. Current health awareness, raised in some degree to the usage of GLP-1 medicines, indicates eggs as a rich-protein, easily digested food that fits into a fitness lifestyle. In this case, global egg consumption could increase at a 4.96% rate until 2032.

In any case, it is important to remember that, despite the potential strength observed in demand trends, prices and revenues in this market tend to be linked to supply constraints, as in the cases observed related to NPAI. Nowadays, as Figure 2 illustrates, egg prices are at a historical high level, reducing since their peak in Q1 2025 but still with room for further reductions.

Alternative protein sources, on the other hand, are under pressure. The USDA reduced beef import projections by 6.1% related to the introduction of import tariffs. At the same time, there is pressure on local production, which already observes an increase in cattle prices and beef for final consumers. Something more or less similar is occurring in the poultry segment. In this way, it has been observed an undergoing demand shift from more expensive proteins to less expensive ones, abnormally improving the demand for eggs and reflecting in average prices. This substitution effect may represent support in the short-term price of eggs. If it seems probable that they will continue to subside, it may not be the case of total collapse.

In such a situation, considering that earnings and cash flow are directly related to price trends in the case of Cal-Maine, as explained, investors may evaluate future cash flow as continuously healthy but reduced in comparison to current levels, directly affecting the true yield generation by the firm. As the most likely scenario is for the continuation of robust cash generation, the final grade for the stock is a hold. Investors focused on dividends may have special attention to Cal-Maine, considering it has great dividend safety and growth at a forward 8.14% yield. As analyzed in Figure 8, the company has enough cash generation to increase cash levels without prejudice to consistent investor remuneration.

Conclusion

Cal-Maine is an option for equity investors in a portfolio logic. Its robust past cash generation may be reduced but keep a still interesting level. Dividend investors may find additional appeal in the stock. In terms of risk management, investors should directly track egg prices and trends, managing the position within portfolios.

data.title: Diamond Hill Mid Cap Strategy Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Commentary

Following a sharp downturn in April in the wake of President Trump’s “Liberation Day” tariffs announcement, markets rose relatively uniformly for the rest of the quarter. For the quarter, US stocks rose approximately 11% (as measured by the Russell 3000 Index). Large-cap stocks bounced the most in the quarter, rising just over 11%, while small and mid caps gained roughly 8.5% each. From a style perspective, growth again outperformed value, which, given the sharp market rebound and seeming improvement in investor sentiment, was not particularly surprising. Large-cap growth stocks rose nearly 18% in the quarter as big technology stocks resumed market leadership; in contrast, large-cap value stocks increased by a more modest 3.8%. Likewise, mid-cap growth stocks were up by more than 18% compared to mid-cap value stocks, which rose by a little over 5%. The gap between small-cap growth and value was the smallest, at +12% versus +5%, respectively.

From a sector perspective, investors’ risk-on attitude seemed to likewise return, with the growthier sectors in the Russell Midcap Index leading, while defensive sectors fell out of favor. Information technology stocks performed best, rising 22%, followed by communication services, which gained almost 16% and industrials, up over 13%. Energy (-5.2%), consumer staples (-1.2%) and real estate (-0.4%) were the index’s only sectors in the red in Q2 — with energy especially hampered by geopolitical upheaval.

2Q25 Russell Midcap Index Sector Returns (%)

As has largely been the case since 2025 began, economic data appear to have a minimal impact on market movements, in contrast to headlines surrounding ostensible winners and losers in the ongoing trade upheaval that has prevailed since President Trump took office in January. In Q2, following Trump’s announced raft of tariffs, many countries sought new trade deals with the US, and several reached significant new agreements, including the UK and China. The US and China agreed in May to cut tariffs on one another for the ensuing 90 days. Since then, progress with China seems to have largely stalled — likewise, progress on a new US-EU deal. However, neither the US nor China has reverted to the prior threatened tariff rates, which were inarguably exorbitant. Meanwhile, bilateral discussions between other countries — including the UK and India and the UK and the EU — seem to have borne fruit, the former trade partners reaching an agreement roughly three years after beginning negotiations and the latter agreeing to reset post-Brexit negotiations, ideally clearing the way for tangible progress toward a lasting trade deal.

In contrast to the market’s reaction, central banks globally have seemingly remained committed to watching and reacting to economic data, with the Federal Reserve holding rates steady in June even amid pressure from President Trump and growing dissension among its ranks regarding whether a rate cut may be appropriate. The Bank of England likewise held rates steady in June. The European Central Bank (ECB), however, cut rates again in June, even as inflation fell below its 1.9% target, though ECB President Christine Lagarde indicated that rate cuts may be at an end.

If anything could steal headlines from all the quarter’s trade developments, it was geopolitics — and indeed, the ongoing Middle East war and the US’s increased role in it were front and center in June. Toward the end of the month, following the expiration of a 60-day window during which Iran could have returned to the negotiating table, President Trump authorized military strikes on three nuclear sites in the country. Iran subsequently launched retaliatory missile strikes against Israeli targets and fired several missiles at a US airbase in Qatar, though the latter missiles had little impact and resulted in no US casualties. Shortly after the US strikes, Trump announced a ceasefire between Iran and Israel and attempted to bring the two sides to the table — efforts which are ongoing as we write, though the truce appears to be holding for now. Meanwhile, the war between Russia and Ukraine continues apace, with the two sides currently seeming as far as they’ve been from either a ceasefire or meaningful progress toward a negotiated peace.

While the selloff in early Q2 created some interesting and compelling investment opportunities, it was undoubtedly a short window in which to capitalize, given the market’s sharp rebound. However, we remain focused on our investment philosophy, which seeks to identify opportunities to invest in companies trading at valuations we believe are disconnected from their underlying fundamentals and long-term growth outlooks. We believe that by focusing in this way, we can continue to add value for our clients in the period ahead, regardless of how markets evolve from here.

Performance Discussion

Though positive on an absolute basis, our portfolio trailed the Russell Midcap Index in Q2. Relative weakness was concentrated in our information technology exposure — both our underweight position in the index’s top-performing sector and our holdings, which, despite positive returns, trailed benchmark peers. Our health care and financials holdings also posed relative headwinds. Conversely, our industrials holdings provided a relative tailwind, as did our overweight to a strong sector. Our utilities holdings also provided a boost to relative returns.

On an individual holdings basis, top contributors to return in Q2 included Red Rock Resorts (RRR) and WESCO (WCC). Red Rock Resorts, a casino operator controlling over half the Las Vegas locals market, has demonstrated the resilience of its business model via a strong start to the year. Coming into Q2, the valuation was attractive — and investors responded accordingly, giving a boost to shares.

Shares of industrial distributor WESCO rose amid a solid demand environment, particularly among data center customers. Long term, we expect WESCO to leverage its significant scale advantage to take share and improve margins. Further, the company is well-positioned to benefit from several secular tailwinds aside from data centers — all factors which the market has not yet fully appreciated.

Other top Q2 individual contributors included Regal Rexnord, Mr. Cooper Group (COOP) and Huntington Ingalls Industries (HII). Power transmission components manufacturer Regal Rexnord has held up well against an uncertain macroeconomic backdrop. Merger synergies, its 80/20 growth initiative and continuous improvement efforts are driving strong margin improvement and better-than-expected organic growth. While somewhat elevated leverage could leave the company vulnerable to further near-term macroeconomic weakness, we believe Regal Rexnord’s self-help initiatives should contribute to ongoing margin improvement and organic growth faster than peers’ — which we don’t believe the current share price reflects.

Shares of mortgage servicing company Mr. Cooper Group rose as investors increasingly appreciate the valuation potential created by Rocket Companies’ announced acquisition of Mr. Cooper Group. US Department of Defense (DOD) and Navy supplier Huntington Ingalls benefited from recent headlines which seem to point to a strong outlook for the US Navy and shipbuilding for the foreseeable future — particularly in terms of the potential for significant congressional budget allocations for destroyers and Virginia- class submarines. We believe Huntington Ingalls remains one of the best positioned defense companies to meet the DOD’s need to prepare for a potential conflict in the Pacific.

Among our bottom Q2 individual contributors were Icon (ICON) and Post Holdings (POST). ICON’s shares declined during the quarter, as contract research organizations (CROs) broadly underperformed amid ongoing uncertainty around the pace and timing of recovery in biotech and pharmaceutical order volumes. Investor sentiment weakened as visibility into normalized growth trajectories remained limited.

Food products manufacturer Post Holdings has faced weaker volumes in recent quarters, particularly in its cereal category — though management has emphasized volume weakness has been broad-based across the packaged foods industry, so Post is not alone on this front. Importantly, fundamentals in other areas of the business generally remain solid.

Other bottom Q2 contributors included Insperity (NSP), Ashland (ASH) and Teleflex (TFX). Insperity provides human resources (HR) and business solutions primarily to US-based small and mid-sized businesses. Shares were pressured in the quarter as higher-than-expected health care costs impacted the company’s insurance book and the company’s worksite employee count grew at a tepid rate. Our confidence in the company’s execution across multiple fronts — including its Workday partnership, ability to grow worksite employees, manage its health insurance book of business and improve margins — has increasingly weakened. Further, the potential for AI disruption is a longer-term risk to Insperity’s worksite employee base. With these factors pressuring the share price and little visibility into a meaningful turnaround over the course of our investing horizon, we exited our position during the quarter, redeploying the proceeds to more attractive risk/reward opportunities.

Specialty chemical manufacturer Ashland faces the potential for a weaker European macroeconomic environment and Chinese construction industry — sentiment-related factors which have weighed more on the share price than they have on fundamentals, which have deteriorated more modestly. For now, we are maintaining our position and believe the company could at some point become attractive to potential acquirers given the discount at which it is currently trading.

Teleflex designs, develops, manufactures and supplies single-use medical devices for common critical care and surgical diagnostic and therapeutic procedures. Shares were pressured in the quarter against the backdrop of concerns about tariffs’ potential impact on the company in the period ahead.

Portfolio Activity

Despite markets’ relatively sharp bounce following April’s downward volatility, we were able to initiate two new positions in the quarter at what we consider compelling valuations: Alaska Air Group (ALK) and FTI Consulting (FCN).

Shares of regional airline Alaska Air Group were pressured meaningfully in the quarter following the tariff announcements, which raised questions among investors about the overall macroeconomic outlook. This gave us an attractive opportunity to reestablish a position in a business we know well — and which we had recently exited after it reached our estimate of intrinsic value — at a compelling valuation.

FTI Consulting provides business advisory services to manage change, mitigate risk and resolve disputes. It has a robust, balanced services lineup spanning procyclical (e.g., M&A advisory work), countercyclical (e.g., restructuring advisory) and acyclical (e.g., forensic accounting and litigation consulting) engagements. The company has improved its scale, geographic reach and service breadth over the past decade or so as the CEO and management team have successfully seeded and grown service areas and geographies while generally reorienting the company’s culture toward organic growth. Near-term disruptions over the past several quarters — which we believe will prove to be largely noise in the long term — gave us an opportunity to initiate a position in what we consider a healthy, solid business run by a capable management team at a compelling price.

We funded these purchases in part with the proceeds from our sale of ski resort owner and operator Vail Resorts (MTN), which we exited in favor of more compelling opportunities elsewhere, in addition to the aforementioned Insperity.

Market Outlook

Equity markets have demonstrated remarkable resilience in 2025, navigating a complex environment shaped by shifting policies, economic uncertainty and evolving investor sentiment. As we look ahead, the interplay between these factors will likely continue to influence market dynamics, presenting both challenges and opportunities for investors.

While near-term economic growth projections remain healthy, signs of a weakening consumer have emerged across various sectors. Given the consumer’s critical role in driving the economy, further softening could pose risks to the high-single-digit corporate earnings growth currently anticipated for the year. Monitoring these trends will be essential as we assess the sustainability of current market expectations.

Investor preferences have also shifted notably this year, reflecting the broader uncertainty in the economic and policy landscape. While defensive and value stocks gained favor earlier in the year, recent trends have highlighted renewed interest in growth-oriented and economically sensitive sectors. These shifts underscore the importance of maintaining a flexible and forward-looking approach to portfolio management, as market leadership may continue to evolve in response to changing conditions.

The selloff earlier this year created a brief but compelling window for investment opportunities, though the subsequent market rebound quickly closed that window. However, we remain steadfast in our commitment to delivering value-added results for our clients. By focusing on companies with strong fundamentals and long-term growth potential, we believe we can achieve returns that exceed the broader market over time.

Looking forward, the path ahead will likely be shaped by a combination of economic resilience, policy developments and shifts in market dynamics. By staying focused on our disciplined investment process, we aim to navigate these complexities and position our portfolio for long-term success.

Team

Chris Welch, CFA, Portfolio Manager

Kristen Sheffield, CFA, CIPM, Portfolio Specialist

(DHCM) is an investment adviser registered with the Securities and Exchange Commission and a wholly owned subsidiary of Diamond Hill Investment Group, Inc.; registration does not imply a certain level of skill or training. DHCM provides investment management services to individuals and institutions through mutual funds, separately managed accounts, collective investment trusts, a private fund, a closed-end interval fund and other pooled vehicles including subadvised funds and model delivery programs. A complete list and description of all composites and policies for valuing investments, calculating performance and preparing GIPS reports is available upon request. To receive a complete list and description of all Diamond Hill composites and/or a GIPS® report, contact Scott Stapleton at 614.255.3329, sstapleton@diamond-hill.com or 325 John H. McConnell Blvd., Suite 200, Columbus, OH 43215. A list of broad distribution pooled funds is available upon request. In addition, a list of limited distribution pooled fund descriptions is available upon request. The Mid Cap Composite is comprised of discretionary, fee-paying, non-wrap accounts managed according to the firm’s Mid Cap equity strategy. The strategy’s investment objective is to achieve long-term capital appreciation by investing in medium market capitalization companies selling for less than our estimate of intrinsic value. Medium market capitalization companies are defined as those companies with a market capitalization between $1.5 billion and $20 billion (or, if greater, the maximum market capitalization of companies generally within the capitalization range of the Russell Midcap Index) at the time of purchase. The composite results reflect the reinvestment of dividends, capital gains and other earnings when appropriate. Composite returns and benchmark returns are presented gross of withholding taxes on dividends, interest income and capital gains. Returns are calculated using US Dollars. Net returns are calculated by reducing the gross returns by the highest stated fee in the composite fee schedule. Only transaction costs are deducted from gross of fees returns. Prior to 30 September 2022, actual fees were used in calculating net returns. All net returns were changed retroactively to reflect the highest fee in the composite fee schedule. The Russell Midcap Index is the benchmark. This index measures the performance of roughly 800 US mid-cap companies. The Russell Midcap Value Index is shown as additional information. This index measures the performance of US mid-cap companies with lower price/book ratios and forecasted growth values. The indexes are unmanaged, market capitalization weighted, include net reinvested dividends, do not reflect fees or expenses (which would lower the return) and are not available for direct investment. The views expressed are those of Diamond Hill as of 30 June 2025 and are subject to change without notice. These opinions are not intended to be a forecast of future events, a guarantee of future results or investment advice. Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal.
