data.title: Brookfield Infrastructure: Boring But Strong Case For Retirement Income
data.description:
I have been covering Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP)(BIP.UN:CA)(BIPC) since early 2024 and issuing quarterly earnings updates on a rather regular basis.

All of my articles have been bullish, as BIP's current income value proposition has remained strong. By the "current income value prop," I mean the following aspects:

  • Material dividend yield.
  • Inflation-linked cash flows.
  • Potential for gradual price appreciation even though the income is clearly the primary return driver.
  • Resilient business model.

All in all, I think that BIP is a security, which simply has to be included in retirement income-seeking investor portfolios. As stated above, the offered yield is decent enough to move the needle, and the fundamentals are defensive enough to provide the necessary safety for retirees and, in general, prudent income investors.

Now, my previous piece on BIP was circulated when the Q1, 2025 report was released. The stock is up by 11%, and the total return figure is 14%.

At the same time, the yield remains enticing - at 5.5%. The P/FFO does not indicate any overvaluation. In fact, I would say that the P/FFO of 9.5x signals that BIP is in a bargain territory.

Let me discuss the Q2, 2025 earnings report and update the thesis accordingly.

Thesis review

Before I jump into the details, let's take a quick look at the chart below:

This chart illustrates how BIP has managed to increase the FFO per share in a very consistent and attractive fashion. This period includes such situations as the oil shock, COVID-19, fears of China slowing down, rapidly rising interest rates etc. Despite all of these ups and downs, the FFO per share figure has ticked up without experiencing any hiccups in the process.

And it is quite important to note that the FFO is expressed here on a per share (or unit) basis, which means that the ~8% CAGR has not been fueled by dilutive equity sales.

If we turn to the Q2, 2025 result, we will simply see a clear continuation of this trend. Namely, in Q2, BIP generated FFO per share of $0.81, which translates to 5% year-over-year growth. Adjusted for the FX swings, the FFO per share is up by 9%. It is totally fine that we see FX-driven fluctuations, which in some quarters introduce positive effects and in some (like this time) negative adjustments. The key is that the underlying business performance remains robust and shows no sign of stopping.

The FFO increase was mostly driven by the combination of the following factors:

  • Embedded CPI escalators.
  • Organic growth.
  • CapEx and M&A-driven growth.

Let me just provide two examples, which capture the business case nicely.

First, the midstream segment generated FFO of $157 million, which is 10% above the FFO result achieved in the same quarter last year. The midstream segment is one of the largest for BIP and is inherently durable with predictable cash flow generation.

Second, the data segment generated $113 million, which is 45% above the figure registered in the same quarter last year. And this is a segment, where BIP has heavily concentrated its CapEx agenda. It will require just two or three years for the data segment to become the largest FFO contributor for BIP. So, effectively, BIP is moving in a direction of having assets that carry relatively rich valuation multiples. Given the aforementioned single digit P/FFO, I think that it would be fair to assume that sooner or later we will be talking about price-return potential more than the dividend case.

Speaking of cash distributions, the 3-year CAGR profile is clearly above the inflation rate (i.e., at ~6%, which goes nicely together with the realized FFO growth). In fact, even though the distribution increases have been material, since the rate of change in the FFO figure has surpassed the dividend hikes, we can now enjoy a growth-accommodative FFO payout of 68%.

Finally, in the Q2 data points we can notice another aspect, which is supportive of sustainable growth. And by sustainable growth I mean CapEx and M&A strategies that are not facilitated at the expense of BIP's investment-grade profile. One thing is the FFO retention that stems from a conservative payout, but it is even more important to factor in the benefits of selected infrastructure project monetizations.

On a YTD basis, BIP has secured $2.4 billion of fresh liquidity through accretive asset sales. To illustrate how all of this is accretive:

  • In Q2, BIP secured a sale of a 23% interest in its Australian export terminal (metallurgical coal export facility), from which BIP is set to collect $280 million at a cumulative return of 22% and a multiple of initially invested capital of 4x.
  • In the same quarter, BIP entered into an agreement to divest partial stakes of its U.K. port operations, from which it will capture about $385 million at an IRR of 19% and a 7.5x multiple of invested capital.

The bottom line

The Q2 earnings data confirm yet again that BIP is a solid retirement income play. The underlying cash flows have kept growing and in a manner that even exceeds the distribution hikes. On top of that, we see a heavier tilt towards the data segment, which is what eventually will provide us with a justified base for expecting a positive valuation reset. Finally, it is also encouraging to see that the growth gets funded by very enticing project sales, which validate the case that BIP should indeed trade at a higher multiple than ~P/FFO 9.5x and, importantly, that the growth does not come at the expense of prudent financial profile.

With this, I am keeping the buy rating unchanged.
